1	# Agent Instructions
2	
3	All agent-facing instructions live in this file. `CLAUDE.md` contains only a
4	reference here. If you are a Claude Code agent, you have already loaded this
5	via `CLAUDE.md`. Other agents: read this file directly.
6	
7	## Build and test
8	
9	```bash
10	# Run unit tests (REQUIRED before every PR)
11	pip install pytest  # if not already installed
12	python3 -m pytest tests/ -v
13	
14	# Validate imports
15	python3 -c "from runner import main"
16	python3 -c "from generate_results import generate_results_md"
17	python3 -c "from test_quality import compute_structural_metrics"
18	python3 -c "from llm_providers import get_provider"
19	
20	# Regenerate all reports
21	python3 generate_results.py --all
22	
23	# Run a benchmark (v4, all tasks/modes/models)
24	python3 runner.py --tasks 11,12,13,15,16,17,18 --modes default,powershell,bash,typescript-bun --models opus,sonnet
25	
26	# Evaluate test quality (structural metrics only)
27	python3 test_quality.py results/2026-04-09_152435
28	
29	# Evaluate test + deliverable quality with the default panel of judges
30	# (Haiku 4.5 via claude-cli + Gemini 3.1 Pro via gemini-cli). Each judge
31	# writes its own cache file: test-quality-{short}.json and
32	# deliverable-quality-{short}.json. 8-worker thread pool by default;
33	# bump `--workers` if your CLIs + account limits allow more concurrency.
34	python3 test_quality.py --llm-judge --deliverable-judge \
35	    --judges haiku45,gemini31pro --workers 8 results/2026-04-17_004319
36	
37	# Re-evaluate with a single judge only (useful for bias cross-checks)
38	python3 test_quality.py --llm-judge --judges haiku45 results/2026-04-17_004319
39	
40	# Build custom act container (optional, eliminates pwsh install overhead)
41	docker build -t act-ubuntu-pwsh:latest -f Dockerfile.act .
42	```
43	
44	## Code style
45	
46	- Python 3.12+. No type stubs or mypy. Use type hints where they aid readability.
47	- Dollar amounts in results.md: round to nearest penny (`.2f`).
48	- Durations in results.md: always in minutes with 1 decimal (`{seconds/60:.1f}min`).
49	- No emojis in code or docs unless the user asks.
50	
51	## Terminology
52	
53	When the runtime wrapper uses `language_mode` (Python-side variable name), the
54	*user-facing* axis is called **language**, never "mode". This covers docs,
55	prompts, report prose, and LLM summaries. The internal field name stays
56	`language_mode` so existing metrics.json readers don't break; everything else
57	says "language" (e.g. "default/Python, bash, powershell, powershell-tool,
58	typescript-bun"). Rationale: "mode" is ambiguous with agent-approval-modes and
59	execution modes; "language" is the concept readers expect.
60	
61	## Repository rules
62	
63	- **No agent-generated `.github/workflows/` at repo root.** Agent workflows only exist inside workspaces under `workspaces/`. The repo's own CI workflow (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`) is the exception.
64	- **Never fix agent-generated code.** The benchmark measures autonomous output. Do not manually fix, edit, or patch workflow files in `workspaces/` or `results/*/generated-code/`.
65	- **`runner.py` observes and records, never intervenes** on agent code or errors.
66	- **Workspaces are throwaway.** Don't commit `workspaces/` contents.
67	- **`results/` is committed.** It contains archived metrics, generated code, and transcripts.
68	- **`CLAUDE.md` is only a pointer.** All instructions go in `AGENTS.md`. Never put substantive content in `CLAUDE.md` — it should only reference this file.
69	
70	## Architecture
71	
72	### Key files
73	
74	- `models.py` — single source of truth for model IDs and token pricing. Update here when Anthropic changes prices.
75	- `runner.py` — benchmark harness. Runs agents via `claude -p`, collects metrics, pushes results. Imports from `models.py` and `generate_results.py`.
76	- `generate_results.py` — generates `results.md` reports and updates `README.md`. Can run standalone: `python3 generate_results.py --all`.
77	- `test_quality.py` — test + deliverable quality evaluation. Structural metrics (always) + panel-of-judges LLM evaluation (`--llm-judge` for test-quality judge, `--deliverable-judge` for workflow+scripts judge). The default panel is Haiku 4.5 + Gemini 3.1 Pro (configured in the module-level `JUDGES` dict). Each judge writes its own per-run cache file; `load_panel_scores(variant_dir, kind)` reads them and returns a mean-aggregated score dict for the reporting layer. Legacy single-Sonnet `*-llm.json` caches still read for backward compat. Imported by `generate_results.py` for the "Test Quality Evaluation" section.
78	- `llm_providers.py` — pluggable LLM provider abstraction for evaluation tasks (see "Adding LLM providers" below). Currently registered: `claude-cli` (pre-authenticated Claude Code CLI), `gemini-cli` (pre-authenticated Gemini CLI, bypasses billing gate), `gemini-api` (google-genai SDK, requires `GEMINI_API_KEY` and a paid-tier Google AI Studio account).
79	- `benchmark-instructions-v*.md` — per-version specs given to agents during runs.
80	- `hooks/syntax-check.py` — PostToolUse hook for syntax/lint checking.
81	- `Dockerfile.act` — custom act container image with pwsh + Pester pre-installed. Build with `docker build -t act-ubuntu-pwsh:latest -f Dockerfile.act .`. Runner.py auto-detects it and injects `.actrc` into workspaces.
82	- `skills/` — agent skills following [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io/specification) spec.
83	
84	### Adding new trap detectors
85	
86	See the docstring on `_detect_traps()` in `generate_results.py`. Each trap needs:
87	a kebab-case name, detection logic over bash_cmds/console/metrics, a time estimate,
88	and an entry in `trap_applicable_mode` if mode-specific.
89	
90	### LLM vs structural discrepancy checks
91	
92	After every report generation (`python3 generate_results.py --all`), check the
93	"LLM vs Structural Discrepancies" section in each `results.md`. Discrepancies
94	are auto-classified by `_find_discrepancies()` in `generate_results.py`:
95	
96	- **counter-gap**: structural metrics are implausibly low (e.g. 0 tests or 0
97	  assertions while LLM scores high). This means `test_quality.py` is missing a
98	  test pattern. **Fix the counter** — read the generated test files to identify
99	  the pattern the counter doesn't recognize, add it to the appropriate
100	  `_count_*()` function and the detection/classification patterns, add unit
101	  tests, and regenerate. Counter-gap discrepancies should not persist across PRs.
102	- **qualitative**: structural metrics look reasonable but the LLM disagrees on
103	  quality. The report includes the LLM's justification (from the `summary`
104	  field in `test-quality-llm.json`). These are expected and acceptable — review
105	  the justification to confirm it's coherent, then leave them.
106	
107	If a **new counter-gap** appears after changing `test_quality.py`, it's a
108	regression. Fix it before merging.
109	
110	### Combined-report invariants (`combine_results.py`)
111	
112	The combined report (e.g. `results/results_<dirA>__<dirB>.md`) pools
113	runs across multiple source directories. A few layout invariants must
114	hold — changes here have broken the generated markdown before, so
115	`tests/test_combine_results.py` guards them:
116	
117	- **No duplicate `(language, variant_disp)` rows in Tiers or
118	  Comparison.** `aggregate_rows` groups by `(language_mode,
119	  model_short, effort_level)` only. CLI versions pool into one row;
120	  `cli_versions` on the row retains the per-CLI breakdown for the
121	  legend to consume. (Previously the aggregate split by CLI, which
122	  rendered as duplicate-looking rows whose Model column matched.)
123	- **CLI Version Legend schema.** Exactly one CLI version per row.
124	  Columns: `Variant label | CLI version | Tasks | Languages`. `Tasks`
125	  and `Languages` each hold either `All` (pair covered every task /
126	  every language observed in the report) or a comma-sorted subset.
127	  The plural header `CLI version(s)` and comma-joined version cells
128	  are the old layout — do not reintroduce.
129	- **Section order in the body:** Scoring → Conclusions → **Judge
130	  Consistency Summary** → Tiers → Comparison → Per-Run → Notes. JCS
131	  is a top-level `##`, not a `### ` under Notes, because readers
132	  benefit from the panel-health verdict before they consume
133	  rankings.
134	- **Quality-score lookup key.** The per-variant score bucket is keyed
135	  by `(language_mode, _label(m))` which includes the `-cli<ver>`
136	  suffix; aggregate lookups must therefore use `r["variant_with_cli"]`
137	  (not `r["variant"]`). A prior regression where the lookup used the
138	  CLI-less `variant` caused every aggregate Tests Quality / Workflow
139	  Craft cell to render as `—` despite per-run scores being present.
140	
141	### Where the Conclusions prose lives
142	
143	The max-effort Opus `## Conclusions` block is produced **only for
144	the combined cross-run report** (`combine_results.py`), not for
145	per-run `results.md` files. `generate_results.py` still invokes the
146	JCS Summary LLM call (cheap) but passes `speed_cost_input=None` so
147	`conclusions_report.generate_conclusions_from_inputs` short-circuits
148	the Conclusions call. Reasoning: comparing a single run directory
149	against itself doesn't surface tradeoffs worth ~$1 of max-effort
150	tokens per regen — the Conclusions prose only reads as useful when
151	multiple run dirs are being compared.
152	
153	If you need per-run prose for some new purpose, plumb through a
154	separate prompt; do not re-enable the merged Conclusions call at the
155	single-run site.
156	
157	### Judge rationale audit (`judge_audit.py`)
158	
159	The combined report includes a `## Judge Audit Outcomes` section
160	that lists every run where the panel judges span ≥ 4 points on a
161	1–5 scale (i.e. Haiku = 1, Gemini = 5). For each flagged row the
162	audit scans each judge's rationale for concrete file-existence
163	claims (see `MISSING_PHRASES` + file-extension regex) and resolves
164	them against the run's `generated-code/` tree. Drop rule:
165	
166	- One judge contradicted → drop that judge's score; panel mean
167	  becomes the other judge's number.
168	- Both contradicted → drop both; panel mean becomes `—` and the
169	  row is excluded from aggregates.
170	- Neither contradicted → keep both.
171	
172	Verdicts persist as `judge-audit-<kind>.json` next to each run's
173	judge caches. `test_quality.load_panel_scores` consumes the verdict
174	automatically, so the Tiers / Comparison / LLM-as-Judge tables
175	above honor the audit with no extra plumbing.
176	
177	### Per-judge prompt addendums
178	
179	`JUDGES[...]` in `test_quality.py` accepts a `prompt_addendum_tests`
180	key. The test-quality evaluator appends it to the shared rubric for
181	that judge alone, so a model-specific steer (e.g. Haiku's
182	missing-file sanity note) doesn't drag the other judges along.
183	`python3 test_quality.py --rejudge haiku45 <run_dir>` refreshes
184	only that judge's cache — handy after tweaking its addendum.
185	
186	### Combined-report parity with per-run reports
187	
188	Per-run `results.md` carries three top-level sections the combined
189	report does not yet replicate in full:
190	
191	| Per-run section                  | Combined report status |
192	|----------------------------------|------------------------|
193	| `## Failed / Timed-Out Runs`     | Ported (2026-04-21)    |
194	| `## Test Quality Evaluation`     | Partial — structural metrics + panel LLM-as-Judge table ported; Correlation and LLM-vs-Structural Discrepancies sub-tables still absent |
195	| `## Savings Analysis` (Hook / Trap / Cache savings) | **Not yet ported.** Depends on per-run hook telemetry + `_detect_traps` output that aren't currently threaded into `combine_results.py`. Readers needing savings data should drop into the per-run `results.md` for now. |
196	
197	When porting the remaining bits, factor the section builders out of
198	`generate_results.py` into module-level helpers so both entry
199	points share a single implementation — the duplication we'd
200	otherwise accrue would drift the two reports out of sync.
201	
202	### Judge consistency summary (prompt hygiene)
203	
204	`JUDGE_CONSISTENCY_SUMMARY_SYSTEM_PROMPT` in
205	`judge_consistency_report.py` forbids unexplained shorthand. If you
206	surface disagreement rows, introduce them with the plain-language gap
207	size (e.g. "the widest disagreements — a judge scoring 1 vs 5, a
208	4-point gap on a 1–5 scale — include …"). Do not coin abbreviations
209	("Span-N", "Δ-N", etc.) that a reader has to decode. Cite specific
210	runs as `task-id-name / language / model-variant` (the same triple
211	the Per-Run table uses).
212	
213	Follow-up analyses of flagged disagreement rows live under
214	`results/analysis/` as dated standalone markdown files (e.g.
215	`judge_disagreement_1-vs-5_2026-04-21.md`). Link them from the JCS
216	section when regenerating if they remain relevant.
217	
218	### Updating model pricing
219	
220	Edit `models.py`. Check https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/about-claude/models and
221	https://www.anthropic.com/pricing. Then run `python3 generate_results.py --all`.
222	
223	### Regenerating reports
224	
225	After changing `generate_results.py`, run:
226	```bash
227	python3 generate_results.py --all
228	```
229	This regenerates `results.md` for every run directory and updates `README.md`.
230	
231	### Adding LLM providers
232	
233	The LLM-as-judge evaluation in `test_quality.py` uses a pluggable provider
234	system defined in `llm_providers.py`. The benchmark runner (`runner.py`) is
235	inherently tied to the Claude Code CLI (it tests CLI-specific features), but
236	the evaluation layer is provider-agnostic.
237	
238	To add a new provider (e.g., Anthropic API, OpenAI, Codex CLI):
239	
240	1. Open `llm_providers.py` and create a class inheriting from `LLMProvider`.
241	2. Implement `is_available()` — return True when the provider can be used.
242	3. Implement `judge(system_prompt, user_message, model)` — return
243	   `{"text": str, "cost_usd": float, "input_tokens": int, "output_tokens": int}`.
244	4. Register it in the `PROVIDERS` dict at the bottom of the file.
245	5. Use it: `python3 test_quality.py --llm-judge --provider your-provider`.
246	
247	See the docstring in `llm_providers.py` for a complete example skeleton.
248	
249	## Before every PR
250	
251	1. **Run all unit tests and verify they pass:**
252	   ```bash
253	   python3 -m pytest tests/ -v
254	   ```
255	   All tests must pass. Do not create or update a PR with failing tests.
256	2. **If you added or changed code, add or update unit tests** in `tests/`.
257	   New functions need test coverage. Changed behavior needs updated assertions.
258	3. Run `python3 generate_results.py --all` and verify no errors.
259	4. **Check for counter-gap discrepancies** in the generated `results.md` files.
260	   If any "Probable counter gaps" appear, fix them in `test_quality.py` before
261	   merging (see "LLM vs structural discrepancy checks" above). Qualitative
262	   disagreements are expected — verify the LLM justification is coherent.
263	5. Verify all import paths work: `python3 -c "from runner import main"`.
264	6. Spot-check a few numbers in results.md against raw metrics.json.
265	7. If you changed architecture or findings, update this file (`AGENTS.md`).
266	8. If you added files or moved things, update the Files table in `README.md`.
267	
268	## Current state (2026-04-13)
269	
270	### v4 benchmark — complete, task 14 archived
271	
272	64/64 runs finished (8 tasks x 4 modes x 2 models). Results in
273	`results/2026-04-09_152435/`. Zero failures, zero timeouts, zero
274	double-result bugs. Total cost $86.90, avg 8.6min/run.
275	
276	v4 added trap-awareness guidance from v3 findings, `shell: pwsh` for
277	PowerShell mode, "limit to 3 act push" instruction, and a custom act
278	Docker image with pwsh/Pester pre-installed. This cut average run time
279	by 24% vs v3 (8.6min vs 11.4min).
280	
281	Post-v4 analysis found Task 14 (Docker Image Tag Generator) redundant
282	with Task 16 (Environment Matrix Generator) across TQ scores, cost, and
283	duration profiles. Task 14 was archived — see `archived-tasks/`. Future
284	runs use 7 tasks (11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 17, 18) x 4 modes x 2 models = 56 runs.
285	
286	### Key findings (v4)
287	
288	- Opus is faster than Sonnet across all modes.
289	- Default mode always chooses Python.
290	- PowerShell/sonnet is the slowest and most expensive combo.
291	- TypeScript hooks have ~50% catch rate but are net negative for Opus
292	  (tsc --noEmit takes 12-21s per Write on large files) and net positive
293	  for Sonnet (smaller writes, 2-3s per check).
294	- PowerShell hooks catch almost nothing (0-4% rate) — net negative.
295	- v4 trap-awareness guidance eliminated the timeout and double-result
296	  bugs that occurred in v3.
297	
298	### Earlier versions
299	
300	- v3: `results/2026-04-08_192624/` — 64 runs, same tasks/modes/models as v4. Had 1 timeout, 3 double-result bugs. Avg 11.4min/run.
301	- v2: `results/2026-04-07_225702/` — 111/144 runs. 18 tasks, modes: default/powershell/powershell-strict/csharp-script. Superseded by v3.
302	- v1: `results/2026-04-02_163146/` — 144 runs, same as v2. Had permission-denial artifacts (88% of errors).
303	- See `design-and-planning-artifacts/` for historical analysis and planning docs.

# Agent Instructions

All agent-facing instructions live in this file. `CLAUDE.md` contains only a
reference here. If you are a Claude Code agent, you have already loaded this
via `CLAUDE.md`. Other agents: read this file directly.

## Build and test

```bash
# Run unit tests (REQUIRED before every PR)
pip install pytest  # if not already installed
python3 -m pytest tests/ -v

# Validate imports
python3 -c "from runner import main"
python3 -c "from generate_results import generate_results_md"
python3 -c "from test_quality import compute_structural_metrics"
python3 -c "from llm_providers import get_provider"

# Regenerate all reports
python3 generate_results.py --all

# Run a benchmark (v4, all tasks/modes/models)
python3 runner.py --tasks 11,12,13,15,16,17,18 --modes default,powershell,bash,typescript-bun --models opus,sonnet

# Evaluate test quality (structural metrics only)
python3 test_quality.py results/2026-04-09_152435

# Evaluate test + deliverable quality with the default panel of judges
# (Haiku 4.5 via claude-cli + Gemini 3.1 Pro via gemini-cli). Each judge
# writes its own cache file: test-quality-{short}.json and
# deliverable-quality-{short}.json. 8-worker thread pool by default;
# bump `--workers` if your CLIs + account limits allow more concurrency.
python3 test_quality.py --llm-judge --deliverable-judge \
    --judges haiku45,gemini31pro --workers 8 results/2026-04-17_004319

# Re-evaluate with a single judge only (useful for bias cross-checks)
python3 test_quality.py --llm-judge --judges haiku45 results/2026-04-17_004319

# Build custom act container (optional, eliminates pwsh install overhead)
docker build -t act-ubuntu-pwsh:latest -f Dockerfile.act .
```

## Code style

- Python 3.12+. No type stubs or mypy. Use type hints where they aid readability.
- Dollar amounts in results.md: round to nearest penny (`.2f`).
- Durations in results.md: always in minutes with 1 decimal (`{seconds/60:.1f}min`).
- No emojis in code or docs unless the user asks.

## Terminology

When the runtime wrapper uses `language_mode` (Python-side variable name), the
*user-facing* axis is called **language**, never "mode". This covers docs,
prompts, report prose, and LLM summaries. The internal field name stays
`language_mode` so existing metrics.json readers don't break; everything else

typescript-bun"). Rationale: "mode" is ambiguous with agent-approval-modes and
execution modes; "language" is the concept readers expect.

## Repository rules

- **No agent-generated `.github/workflows/` at repo root.** Agent workflows only exist inside workspaces under `workspaces/`. The repo's own CI workflow (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`) is the exception.
- **Never fix agent-generated code.** The benchmark measures autonomous output. Do not manually fix, edit, or patch workflow files in `workspaces/` or `results/*/generated-code/`.
- **`runner.py` observes and records, never intervenes** on agent code or errors.
- **Workspaces are throwaway.** Don't commit `workspaces/` contents.
- **`results/` is committed.** It contains archived metrics, generated code, and transcripts.
- **`CLAUDE.md` is only a pointer.** All instructions go in `AGENTS.md`. Never put substantive content in `CLAUDE.md` — it should only reference this file.

## Architecture

### Key files

- `models.py` — single source of truth for model IDs and token pricing. Update here when Anthropic changes prices.
- `runner.py` — benchmark harness. Runs agents via `claude -p`, collects metrics, pushes results. Imports from `models.py` and `generate_results.py`.
- `generate_results.py` — generates `results.md` reports and updates `README.md`. Can run standalone: `python3 generate_results.py --all`.
- `test_quality.py` — test + deliverable quality evaluation. Structural metrics (always) + panel-of-judges LLM evaluation (`--llm-judge` for test-quality judge, `--deliverable-judge` for workflow+scripts judge). The default panel is Haiku 4.5 + Gemini 3.1 Pro (configured in the module-level `JUDGES` dict). Each judge writes its own per-run cache file; `load_panel_scores(variant_dir, kind)` reads them and returns a mean-aggregated score dict for the reporting layer. Legacy single-Sonnet `*-llm.json` caches still read for backward compat. Imported by `generate_results.py` for the "Test Quality Evaluation" section.
- `llm_providers.py` — pluggable LLM provider abstraction for evaluation tasks (see "Adding LLM providers" below). Currently registered: `claude-cli` (pre-authenticated Claude Code CLI), `gemini-cli` (pre-authenticated Gemini CLI, bypasses billing gate), `gemini-api` (google-genai SDK, requires `GEMINI_API_KEY` and a paid-tier Google AI Studio account).
- `benchmark-instructions-v*.md` — per-version specs given to agents during runs.
- `hooks/syntax-check.py` — PostToolUse hook for syntax/lint checking.
- `Dockerfile.act` — custom act container image with pwsh + Pester pre-installed. Build with `docker build -t act-ubuntu-pwsh:latest -f Dockerfile.act .`. Runner.py auto-detects it and injects `.actrc` into workspaces.
- `skills/` — agent skills following [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io/specification) spec.

### Adding new trap detectors

See the docstring on `_detect_traps()` in `generate_results.py`. Each trap needs:
a kebab-case name, detection logic over bash_cmds/console/metrics, a time estimate,
and an entry in `trap_applicable_mode` if mode-specific.

  the justification to confirm it's coherent, then leave them.

If a **new counter-gap** appears after changing `test_quality.py`, it's a
regression. Fix it before merging.

### Combined-report invariants (`combine_results.py`)

The combined report (e.g. `results/results_<dirA>__<dirB>.md`) pools

  rendered as duplicate-looking rows whose Model column matched.)
- **CLI Version Legend schema.** Exactly one CLI version per row.
  Columns: `Variant label | CLI version | Tasks | Languages`. `Tasks`
  and `Languages` each hold either `All` (pair covered every task /
  every language observed in the report) or a comma-sorted subset.
  The plural header `CLI version(s)` and comma-joined version cells
  are the old layout — do not reintroduce.
- **Section order in the body:** Scoring → Conclusions → **Judge

  is a top-level `##`, not a `### ` under Notes, because readers
  benefit from the panel-health verdict before they consume
  rankings.
- **Quality-score lookup key.** The per-variant score bucket is keyed
  by `(language_mode, _label(m))` which includes the `-cli<ver>`
  suffix; aggregate lookups must therefore use `r["variant_with_cli"]`
  (not `r["variant"]`). A prior regression where the lookup used the
  CLI-less `variant` caused every aggregate Tests Quality / Workflow
  Craft cell to render as `—` despite per-run scores being present.

### Where the Conclusions prose lives

The max-effort Opus `## Conclusions` block is produced **only for
the combined cross-run report** (`combine_results.py`), not for
per-run `results.md` files. `generate_results.py` still invokes the
JCS Summary LLM call (cheap) but passes `speed_cost_input=None` so
`conclusions_report.generate_conclusions_from_inputs` short-circuits
the Conclusions call. Reasoning: comparing a single run directory
against itself doesn't surface tradeoffs worth ~$1 of max-effort
tokens per regen — the Conclusions prose only reads as useful when
multiple run dirs are being compared.

If you need per-run prose for some new purpose, plumb through a
separate prompt; do not re-enable the merged Conclusions call at the
single-run site.

### Judge rationale audit (`judge_audit.py`)

The combined report includes a `## Judge Audit Outcomes` section
that lists every run where the panel judges span ≥ 4 points on a
1–5 scale (i.e. Haiku = 1, Gemini = 5). For each flagged row the
audit scans each judge's rationale for concrete file-existence
claims (see `MISSING_PHRASES` + file-extension regex) and resolves
them against the run's `generated-code/` tree. Drop rule:

- One judge contradicted → drop that judge's score; panel mean
  becomes the other judge's number.
- Both contradicted → drop both; panel mean becomes `—` and the
  row is excluded from aggregates.
- Neither contradicted → keep both.

Verdicts persist as `judge-audit-<kind>.json` next to each run's
judge caches. `test_quality.load_panel_scores` consumes the verdict
automatically, so the Tiers / Comparison / LLM-as-Judge tables
above honor the audit with no extra plumbing.

### Per-judge prompt addendums

`JUDGES[...]` in `test_quality.py` accepts a `prompt_addendum_tests`
key. The test-quality evaluator appends it to the shared rubric for
that judge alone, so a model-specific steer (e.g. Haiku's
missing-file sanity note) doesn't drag the other judges along.
`python3 test_quality.py --rejudge haiku45 <run_dir>` refreshes
only that judge's cache — handy after tweaking its addendum.

### Combined-report parity with per-run reports

Per-run `results.md` carries three top-level sections the combined
report does not yet replicate in full:

| Per-run section                  | Combined report status |
|----------------------------------|------------------------|
| `## Failed / Timed-Out Runs`     | Ported (2026-04-21)    |
| `## Test Quality Evaluation`     | Partial — structural metrics + panel LLM-as-Judge table ported; Correlation and LLM-vs-Structural Discrepancies sub-tables still absent |
| `## Savings Analysis` (Hook / Trap / Cache savings) | **Not yet ported.** Depends on per-run hook telemetry + `_detect_traps` output that aren't currently threaded into `combine_results.py`. Readers needing savings data should drop into the per-run `results.md` for now. |

When porting the remaining bits, factor the section builders out of
`generate_results.py` into module-level helpers so both entry
points share a single implementation — the duplication we'd
otherwise accrue would drift the two reports out of sync.

### Judge consistency summary (prompt hygiene)

`JUDGE_CONSISTENCY_SUMMARY_SYSTEM_PROMPT` in
`judge_consistency_report.py` forbids unexplained shorthand. If you
surface disagreement rows, introduce them with the plain-language gap
size (e.g. "the widest disagreements — a judge scoring 1 vs 5, a
4-point gap on a 1–5 scale — include …"). Do not coin abbreviations
("Span-N", "Δ-N", etc.) that a reader has to decode. Cite specific
runs as `task-id-name / language / model-variant` (the same triple
the Per-Run table uses).

Follow-up analyses of flagged disagreement rows live under
`results/analysis/` as dated standalone markdown files (e.g.
`judge_disagreement_1-vs-5_2026-04-21.md`). Link them from the JCS
section when regenerating if they remain relevant.

### Updating model pricing

Edit `models.py`. Check https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/about-claude/models and
https://www.anthropic.com/pricing. Then run `python3 generate_results.py --all`.

### Regenerating reports

After changing `generate_results.py`, run:
```bash
python3 generate_results.py --all
```
This regenerates `results.md` for every run directory and updates `README.md`.

### Adding LLM providers

The LLM-as-judge evaluation in `test_quality.py` uses a pluggable provider
system defined in `llm_providers.py`. The benchmark runner (`runner.py`) is
inherently tied to the Claude Code CLI (it tests CLI-specific features), but
the evaluation layer is provider-agnostic.

To add a new provider (e.g., Anthropic API, OpenAI, Codex CLI):

1. Open `llm_providers.py` and create a class inheriting from `LLMProvider`.
2. Implement `is_available()` — return True when the provider can be used.
3. Implement `judge(system_prompt, user_message, model)` — return
   `{"text": str, "cost_usd": float, "input_tokens": int, "output_tokens": int}`.
4. Register it in the `PROVIDERS` dict at the bottom of the file.
5. Use it: `python3 test_quality.py --llm-judge --provider your-provider`.

See the docstring in `llm_providers.py` for a complete example skeleton.

## Before every PR

1. **Run all unit tests and verify they pass:**
   ```bash
   python3 -m pytest tests/ -v
   ```
   All tests must pass. Do not create or update a PR with failing tests.
2. **If you added or changed code, add or update unit tests** in `tests/`.
   New functions need test coverage. Changed behavior needs updated assertions.
3. Run `python3 generate_results.py --all` and verify no errors.
4. **Check for counter-gap discrepancies** in the generated `results.md` files.
   If any "Probable counter gaps" appear, fix them in `test_quality.py` before
   merging (see "LLM vs structural discrepancy checks" above). Qualitative
   disagreements are expected — verify the LLM justification is coherent.
5. Verify all import paths work: `python3 -c "from runner import main"`.
6. Spot-check a few numbers in results.md against raw metrics.json.
7. If you changed architecture or findings, update this file (`AGENTS.md`).
8. If you added files or moved things, update the Files table in `README.md`.

## Current state (2026-04-13)

### v4 benchmark — complete, task 14 archived

64/64 runs finished (8 tasks x 4 modes x 2 models). Results in
`results/2026-04-09_152435/`. Zero failures, zero timeouts, zero
double-result bugs. Total cost $86.90, avg 8.6min/run.

v4 added trap-awareness guidance from v3 findings, `shell: pwsh` for
PowerShell mode, "limit to 3 act push" instruction, and a custom act
Docker image with pwsh/Pester pre-installed. This cut average run time
by 24% vs v3 (8.6min vs 11.4min).

Post-v4 analysis found Task 14 (Docker Image Tag Generator) redundant
with Task 16 (Environment Matrix Generator) across TQ scores, cost, and
duration profiles. Task 14 was archived — see `archived-tasks/`. Future
runs use 7 tasks (11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 17, 18) x 4 modes x 2 models = 56 runs.

### Key findings (v4)

- Opus is faster than Sonnet across all modes.
- Default mode always chooses Python.
- PowerShell/sonnet is the slowest and most expensive combo.
- TypeScript hooks have ~50% catch rate but are net negative for Opus
  (tsc --noEmit takes 12-21s per Write on large files) and net positive
  for Sonnet (smaller writes, 2-3s per check).
- PowerShell hooks catch almost nothing (0-4% rate) — net negative.
- v4 trap-awareness guidance eliminated the timeout and double-result
  bugs that occurred in v3.

### Earlier versions

- v3: `results/2026-04-08_192624/` — 64 runs, same tasks/modes/models as v4. Had 1 timeout, 3 double-result bugs. Avg 11.4min/run.
- v2: `results/2026-04-07_225702/` — 111/144 runs. 18 tasks, modes: default/powershell/powershell-strict/csharp-script. Superseded by v3.
- v1: `results/2026-04-02_163146/` — 144 runs, same as v2. Had permission-denial artifacts (88% of errors).
- See `design-and-planning-artifacts/` for historical analysis and planning docs.

content.content:
1	# Benchmark Instructions v4 — GitHub Actions
2	
3	**Version**: v4
4	**Date**: 2026-04-09
5	**Purpose**: Measure how well Claude Code agents perform GitHub Actions workflow authoring tasks across different language constraints, with trap-awareness guidance from v3 findings.
6	
7	**Changes from v3**:
8	- Added "Common Pitfalls" section with guidance derived from v3 trap analysis (115min, 15.8% of v3 time lost to traps)
9	- Act container now has pwsh and Pester pre-installed (matching real GitHub runners). Agents still need an install step for workflows that will run on vanilla act, but the benchmarking container already has them.
10	- GHA workflow addendum expanded with `shell: pwsh` guidance for PowerShell mode
11	- All other methodology, tasks, modes, and models are unchanged from v3
12	
13	## Methodology
14	
15	### Overview
16	
17	Each task is executed by a Claude Code agent under 4 different language constraints ("modes") and with 2 different models, yielding **8 runs per task**. Agents receive only the prompt -- no conversation context, no prior results. Each task requires the agent to produce:
18	
19	1. **A script** implementing the task logic in the constrained language
20	2. **Tests** for the script logic using the appropriate framework
21	3. **A GitHub Actions workflow file** (`.github/workflows/<name>.yml`) that integrates the script into a CI/CD pipeline
22	
23	### Models
24	
25	| Model ID | Short Name | Input $/Mtok | Output $/Mtok | Cache Read $/Mtok | Cache Write $/Mtok |
26	|----------|------------|-------------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|
27	| claude-opus-4-6 | opus | $15.00 | $75.00 | $1.50 | $18.75 |
28	| claude-sonnet-4-6 | sonnet | $3.00 | $15.00 | $0.30 | $3.75 |
29	
30	### Language Modes
31	
32	1. **default** -- No language specified. The agent chooses whatever language it considers best. Baseline.
33	2. **powershell** -- The agent must use PowerShell. Tests with non-default but well-known scripting language.
34	3. **bash** -- The agent must use Bash. Scripts run with `bash script.sh`, tests use `bats-core`. The "is this really just a shell script?" control case.
35	4. **typescript-bun** -- The agent must use TypeScript with Bun runtime. Scripts run with `bun run app.ts`, tests use `bun test` (built-in test runner). Zero-config like Python but typed.
36	
37	### TDD Requirement
38	
39	Every prompt instructs the agent to use **red/green TDD**:
40	1. Write a failing test first.
41	2. Write the minimum code to make it pass.
42	3. Refactor.
43	4. Repeat for each piece of functionality.
44	5. Create mocks and test fixtures as necessary.
45	
46	### GitHub Actions Workflow Requirement
47	
48	Create a GitHub Actions workflow file at `.github/workflows/<task-name>.yml` that uses your script in a real CI/CD pipeline. The workflow must:
49	- Use appropriate trigger events (push, pull_request, schedule, workflow_dispatch, etc.)
50	- Reference your script correctly
51	- Pass `actionlint` validation (valid YAML, valid action references, correct syntax)
52	- Include appropriate permissions, environment variables, and job dependencies
53	- Actually run successfully when executed locally with `act` (nektos/act) in a Docker container
54	
55	Design your workflow so its steps work in an isolated Docker container -- use `actions/checkout@v4`, install dependencies your script needs, and run it. Avoid steps requiring external services or secrets without sensible defaults.
56	
57	### Workflow Validation
58	
59	Run `actionlint .github/workflows/<task-name>.yml` and fix any errors. `actionlint` is pre-installed. Iterate until it passes cleanly.
60	
61	### All Tests Must Run Through Act
62	
63	Every single test case must execute through the GitHub Actions workflow via `act`. Do NOT test your script directly -- all testing goes through the pipeline.
64	
65	Structure your workflow to accept test fixture data and produce verifiable output. Your test harness must:
66	1. For each test case: set up a temp git repo with your project files + that case's fixture data, run `act push --rm`, capture the output
67	2. Save all act output to `act-result.txt` in the current working directory (append each test case's output, clearly delimited)
68	3. Assert that act exited with code 0 for each case
69	4. Parse the act output and assert on EXACT EXPECTED VALUES -- not just that output appeared, but that it matches the known-good result for that test case's input. For example: if your workflow bumps version 1.1.0 with a feat commit, assert the output contains exactly "1.2.0", not just "a version number"
70	5. Assert every job shows "Job succeeded"
71	
72	The `act-result.txt` file MUST exist when done. It is a required artifact. `act` and Docker are pre-installed.
73	
74	### Workflow Structure Tests (Also Required)
75	
76	- Parse the YAML and check expected structure (triggers, jobs, steps)
77	- Verify the workflow references your script files correctly (paths exist)
78	- Verify actionlint passes (assert exit code 0)
79	
80	### Pre-installed Tools
81	
82	The following tools are pre-installed in the benchmark environment:
83	- **Python 3** with `pytest`
84	- **PowerShell** (`pwsh`) with **Pester** testing framework
85	- **Bun** (TypeScript runtime with built-in test runner)
86	- **Bash** with **bats-core** testing framework
87	- **actionlint** (GitHub Actions workflow linter)
88	- **shellcheck** (shell script static analysis)
89	- **act** (nektos/act — runs GitHub Actions workflows locally in Docker)
90	- **Docker** (container runtime, used by act)
91	
92	The act Docker container also has `pwsh` and `Pester` pre-installed (matching real GitHub-hosted runners). Your workflow's install-powershell step will be a near-instant no-op. You should still include it for portability, but it won't slow things down.
93	
94	Agents should NOT attempt to install these tools -- they are already available.
95	
96	### Syntax/Lint Hooks
97	
98	All runs have PostToolUse hooks enabled that automatically check files on Write/Edit:
99	- `.py` files: `python3 -m py_compile` for syntax errors
100	- `.sh` files: `bash -n` for syntax errors, `shellcheck` for lint
101	- `.ts` files: `bunx tsc --noEmit` for type errors
102	- `.ps1` files: `Invoke-ScriptAnalyzer` for PowerShell analysis
103	- `.yml` files in `.github/workflows/`: `actionlint` for workflow validation
104	
105	Diagnostics appear as additional context before the agent's next turn, enabling faster error correction.
106	
107	### Permissions
108	
109	All tool permissions are bypassed (`--dangerously-skip-permissions`). Agents have full access to all tools without approval prompts.
110	
111	### Execution
112	
113	- Tasks run **sequentially** (one at a time).
114	- Each agent runs in an **isolated workspace directory** containing only a copy of this instructions file.
115	- No conversation context or prior run data is passed to the agent.
116	
117	---
118	
119	## Common Pitfalls
120	
121	The following patterns were observed in v3 runs wasting significant agent time. Agents are encouraged to avoid them:
122	
123	### PowerShell: Use `shell: pwsh` in workflow steps
124	
125	When writing GitHub Actions workflow YAML for PowerShell mode, use `shell: pwsh` on your `run:` steps instead of invoking `pwsh -Command "..."` or `pwsh -File ...` from a bash shell. The `shell: pwsh` directive works correctly in act containers and avoids:
126	- Bash parser errors when PowerShell syntax (like `@'...'@` heredocs) leaks into bash
127	- Dollar-sign escaping issues (`\$` needed for every PS variable inside bash strings)
128	- Quoting/visibility problems when passing structured data (JSON) through bash to pwsh
129	- `$MyInvocation.ScriptName` being empty under `pwsh -File` invocation
130	
131	Example -- prefer this:
132	```yaml
133	    - name: Run script
134	      shell: pwsh
135	      run: |
136	        ./MyScript.ps1 -Config config.json
137	```
138	
139	Over this:
140	```yaml
141	    - name: Run script
142	      run: |
143	        pwsh -File ./MyScript.ps1 -Config config.json
144	```
145	
146	### Act: Limit `act push` invocations
147	
148	Each `act push` run takes 30-90 seconds (container startup + workflow execution). In v3, agents that ran `act push` more than twice spent 15+ minutes on repeated debugging loops. Strategy:
149	1. Get the workflow right with `actionlint` first (instant validation)
150	2. Run `act push` once to verify
151	3. If it fails, read the error carefully and fix the root cause before re-running
152	4. Avoid running `act push` more than 3 times total
153	
154	### Fixtures: Design test data up front
155	
156	Agents that wrote, broke, and rewrote test fixtures (4+ fixture-related commands) lost 1-5 minutes per run. Design your fixture data before writing tests -- think through the edge cases once, create all fixtures, then write tests against them.
157	
158	### Test reruns: Fix the code, not the test command
159	
160	Running the same test command 4+ times without changing the underlying code is a signal you're not diagnosing the root cause. Read the error output carefully, identify what's wrong, fix it, then re-run once.
161	
162	### TypeScript: Expect type error hook cycles
163	
164	The `tsc --noEmit` hook catches type errors on every Write/Edit. This is net-positive (53% catch rate) but each fix cycle takes ~12 seconds. To minimize cycles: use explicit types from the start, avoid `any`, and check your types mentally before writing.
165	
166	### Workflow: Validate with actionlint before act
167	
168	`actionlint` runs instantly and catches most YAML/syntax issues. Always run it before attempting `act push`. Agents that needed 3+ actionlint runs to pass wasted time on iterative fixes that could have been caught by reading the error messages more carefully.
169	
170	---
171	
172	## Tasks
173	
174	### Task 11: Semantic Version Bumper
175	**Category**: GitHub Actions / Release Automation
176	
177	Parse a version file (or package.json) containing a semantic version string, determine the next version based on conventional commit messages (feat -> minor, fix -> patch, breaking -> major), update the version file, generate a changelog entry from the commits, and output the new version. Create mock commit logs as test fixtures.
178	
179	### Task 12: PR Label Assigner
180	**Category**: GitHub Actions / Triage
181	
182	Given a list of changed file paths (simulating a PR's changed files), apply labels based on configurable path-to-label mapping rules (e.g., docs/** -> documentation, src/api/** -> api, *.test.* -> tests). Support glob patterns, multiple labels per file, and priority ordering when rules conflict. Output the final label set. Mock the file list for testing.
183	
184	### Task 13: Dependency License Checker
185	**Category**: GitHub Actions / Compliance
186	
187	Parse a dependency manifest (package.json, requirements.txt, or similar), extract dependency names and versions, check each against an allow-list and deny-list of licenses (provided as config), and generate a compliance report listing each dependency's license status (approved, denied, unknown). Mock the license lookup for testing.
188	
189	### ~~Task 14: Docker Image Tag Generator~~ (Archived)
190	**Category**: GitHub Actions / CI/CD
191	**Status**: Archived after v4 — redundant with Task 16. See [archived-tasks/14-docker-image-tag-generator.md](archived-tasks/14-docker-image-tag-generator.md).
192	
193	Given git context (branch name, commit SHA, tags, PR number -- all provided as mock inputs), generate appropriate Docker image tags following common conventions: latest for main, pr-{number} for PRs, v{semver} for tags, {branch}-{short-sha} for feature branches. Handle tag sanitization (lowercase, no special chars). Output the tag list.
194	
195	### Task 15: Test Results Aggregator
196	**Category**: GitHub Actions / CI Reporting
197	
198	Parse test result files in multiple formats (JUnit XML, and JSON), aggregate results across multiple files (simulating a matrix build), compute totals (passed, failed, skipped, duration), identify flaky tests (passed in some runs, failed in others), and generate a markdown summary suitable for a GitHub Actions job summary. Create sample test result files as fixtures.
199	
200	### Task 16: Environment Matrix Generator
201	**Category**: GitHub Actions / CI Configuration
202	
203	Given a configuration describing OS options, language versions, and feature flags, generate a build matrix (as JSON) suitable for GitHub Actions strategy.matrix. Support include/exclude rules, max-parallel limits, and fail-fast configuration. Validate that the matrix doesn't exceed a maximum size. Output the complete matrix JSON.
204	
205	### Task 17: Artifact Cleanup Script
206	**Category**: GitHub Actions / Maintenance
207	
208	Given a list of artifacts with metadata (name, size, creation date, workflow run ID -- provided as mock data), apply retention policies (max age, max total size, keep-latest-N per workflow), determine which artifacts to delete, and generate a deletion plan with a summary (total space reclaimed, artifacts retained vs deleted). Support dry-run mode.
209	
210	### Task 18: Secret Rotation Validator
211	**Category**: GitHub Actions / Security
212	
213	Given a configuration of secrets with metadata (name, last-rotated date, rotation policy in days, required-by services -- all mock data), identify secrets that are expired or expiring within a configurable warning window, generate a rotation report, and output notifications grouped by urgency (expired, warning, ok). Support multiple output formats (markdown table, JSON).
214	
215	---
216	
217	## Prompt Templates
218	
219	### Default Mode
220	```
221	You are completing a scripting task. Choose whatever programming language you think is best for this task.
222	
223	TASK: {task_description}
224	
225	REQUIREMENTS:
226	1. Use red/green TDD methodology: write a failing test FIRST, then write the minimum code to make it pass, then refactor. Repeat for each piece of functionality.
227	2. Create mocks and test fixtures as necessary for testability.
228	3. All tests must be runnable and must pass at the end.
229	4. Include clear comments explaining your approach.
230	5. Handle errors gracefully with meaningful error messages.
231	
232	Create your solution in the current working directory. Start by writing your first failing test.
233	```
234	
235	### PowerShell Mode
236	```
237	You are completing a scripting task. You MUST use PowerShell as your implementation language.
238	
239	TASK: {task_description}
240	
241	REQUIREMENTS:
242	1. Use red/green TDD methodology: write a failing test FIRST, then write the minimum code to make it pass, then refactor. Repeat for each piece of functionality.
243	2. Create mocks and test fixtures as necessary for testability. Use Pester as the testing framework.
244	3. All tests must be runnable with `Invoke-Pester` and must pass at the end.
245	4. Include clear comments explaining your approach.
246	5. Handle errors gracefully with meaningful error messages.
247	
248	Create your solution in the current working directory. Start by writing your first failing test.
249	```
250	
251	### Bash Mode
252	```
253	You are completing a scripting task. You MUST use Bash as your implementation language.
254	
255	TASK: {task_description}
256	
257	REQUIREMENTS:
258	1. Use red/green TDD methodology: write a failing test FIRST, then write the minimum code to make it pass, then refactor. Repeat for each piece of functionality.
259	2. Create mocks and test fixtures as necessary for testability. Use bats-core (bats) as the testing framework.
260	3. All tests must be runnable with `bats` and must pass at the end.
261	4. Include clear comments explaining your approach.
262	5. Handle errors gracefully with meaningful error messages.
263	6. Use `#!/usr/bin/env bash` shebang. Scripts must pass `shellcheck` and `bash -n` syntax validation.
264	
265	Create your solution in the current working directory. Start by writing your first failing test.
266	```
267	
268	### TypeScript-Bun Mode
269	```
270	You are completing a scripting task. You MUST use TypeScript with Bun as your implementation language and runtime.
271	
272	TASK: {task_description}
273	
274	REQUIREMENTS:
275	1. Use red/green TDD methodology: write a failing test FIRST, then write the minimum code to make it pass, then refactor. Repeat for each piece of functionality.
276	2. Create mocks and test fixtures as necessary for testability. Use Bun's built-in test runner (`bun test`).
277	3. All tests must be runnable with `bun test` and must pass at the end.
278	4. Include clear comments explaining your approach.
279	5. Handle errors gracefully with meaningful error messages.
280	6. Use TypeScript features: explicit types, interfaces, and type annotations. Run scripts with `bun run <file>.ts`.
281	
282	Create your solution in the current working directory. Start by writing your first failing test.
283	```
284	
285	### GHA Workflow Addendum (appended to all prompts for tasks 11-18)
286	```
287	GITHUB ACTIONS REQUIREMENT:
288	In addition to the script and tests, create a GitHub Actions workflow file at
289	.github/workflows/<task-name>.yml that would use your script in a real CI/CD pipeline.
290	The workflow must:
291	- Use appropriate trigger events (push, pull_request, schedule, workflow_dispatch, etc.)
292	- Reference your script correctly
293	- Pass actionlint validation (valid YAML, valid action references, correct syntax)
294	- Include appropriate permissions, environment variables, and job dependencies
295	
296	The workflow WILL be executed in a Docker container via `act push` after you finish.
297	It must complete without errors. Design your workflow so that its steps work in an
298	isolated container environment — use `actions/checkout@v4`, install any dependencies
299	your script needs, and run the script. Avoid steps that require external services or
300	secrets unless they have sensible defaults/fallbacks.
301	
302	IMPORTANT for PowerShell mode: Use `shell: pwsh` on your workflow run: steps instead
303	of invoking `pwsh -Command` or `pwsh -File` from bash. This avoids escaping issues
304	and works correctly in act containers. pwsh and Pester are pre-installed in the

# Benchmark Instructions v4 — GitHub Actions

**Version**: v4
**Date**: 2026-04-09
**Purpose**: Measure how well Claude Code agents perform GitHub Actions workflow authoring tasks across different language constraints, with trap-awareness guidance from v3 findings.

**Changes from v3**:
- Added "Common Pitfalls" section with guidance derived from v3 trap analysis (115min, 15.8% of v3 time lost to traps)
- Act container now has pwsh and Pester pre-installed (matching real GitHub runners). Agents still need an install step for workflows that will run on vanilla act, but the benchmarking container already has them.
- GHA workflow addendum expanded with `shell: pwsh` guidance for PowerShell mode
- All other methodology, tasks, modes, and models are unchanged from v3

## Methodology

### Overview

Each task is executed by a Claude Code agent under 4 different language constraints ("modes") and with 2 different models, yielding **8 runs per task**. Agents receive only the prompt -- no conversation context, no prior results. Each task requires the agent to produce:

1. **A script** implementing the task logic in the constrained language
2. **Tests** for the script logic using the appropriate framework
3. **A GitHub Actions workflow file** (`.github/workflows/<name>.yml`) that integrates the script into a CI/CD pipeline

### Models

| Model ID | Short Name | Input $/Mtok | Output $/Mtok | Cache Read $/Mtok | Cache Write $/Mtok |
|----------|------------|-------------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| claude-opus-4-6 | opus | $15.00 | $75.00 | $1.50 | $18.75 |
| claude-sonnet-4-6 | sonnet | $3.00 | $15.00 | $0.30 | $3.75 |

### Language Modes

1. **default** -- No language specified. The agent chooses whatever language it considers best. Baseline.
2. **powershell** -- The agent must use PowerShell. Tests with non-default but well-known scripting language.
3. **bash** -- The agent must use Bash. Scripts run with `bash script.sh`, tests use `bats-core`. The "is this really just a shell script?" control case.
4. **typescript-bun** -- The agent must use TypeScript with Bun runtime. Scripts run with `bun run app.ts`, tests use `bun test` (built-in test runner). Zero-config like Python but typed.

### TDD Requirement

Every prompt instructs the agent to use **red/green TDD**:
1. Write a failing test first.
2. Write the minimum code to make it pass.
3. Refactor.
4. Repeat for each piece of functionality.
5. Create mocks and test fixtures as necessary.

### GitHub Actions Workflow Requirement

Create a GitHub Actions workflow file at `.github/workflows/<task-name>.yml` that uses your script in a real CI/CD pipeline. The workflow must:
- Use appropriate trigger events (push, pull_request, schedule, workflow_dispatch, etc.)
- Reference your script correctly
- Pass `actionlint` validation (valid YAML, valid action references, correct syntax)
- Include appropriate permissions, environment variables, and job dependencies
- Actually run successfully when executed locally with `act` (nektos/act) in a Docker container

Design your workflow so its steps work in an isolated Docker container -- use `actions/checkout@v4`, install dependencies your script needs, and run it. Avoid steps requiring external services or secrets without sensible defaults.

### Workflow Validation

Run `actionlint .github/workflows/<task-name>.yml` and fix any errors. `actionlint` is pre-installed. Iterate until it passes cleanly.

### All Tests Must Run Through Act

Every single test case must execute through the GitHub Actions workflow via `act`. Do NOT test your script directly -- all testing goes through the pipeline.

Structure your workflow to accept test fixture data and produce verifiable output. Your test harness must:
1. For each test case: set up a temp git repo with your project files + that case's fixture data, run `act push --rm`, capture the output
2. Save all act output to `act-result.txt` in the current working directory (append each test case's output, clearly delimited)
3. Assert that act exited with code 0 for each case
4. Parse the act output and assert on EXACT EXPECTED VALUES -- not just that output appeared, but that it matches the known-good result for that test case's input. For example: if your workflow bumps version 1.1.0 with a feat commit, assert the output contains exactly "1.2.0", not just "a version number"
5. Assert every job shows "Job succeeded"

The `act-result.txt` file MUST exist when done. It is a required artifact. `act` and Docker are pre-installed.

### Workflow Structure Tests (Also Required)

- Parse the YAML and check expected structure (triggers, jobs, steps)
- Verify the workflow references your script files correctly (paths exist)
- Verify actionlint passes (assert exit code 0)

### Pre-installed Tools

The following tools are pre-installed in the benchmark environment:
- **Python 3** with `pytest`
- **PowerShell** (`pwsh`) with **Pester** testing framework
- **Bun** (TypeScript runtime with built-in test runner)
- **Bash** with **bats-core** testing framework
- **actionlint** (GitHub Actions workflow linter)
- **shellcheck** (shell script static analysis)
- **act** (nektos/act — runs GitHub Actions workflows locally in Docker)
- **Docker** (container runtime, used by act)

The act Docker container also has `pwsh` and `Pester` pre-installed (matching real GitHub-hosted runners). Your workflow's install-powershell step will be a near-instant no-op. You should still include it for portability, but it won't slow things down.

Agents should NOT attempt to install these tools -- they are already available.

### Syntax/Lint Hooks

All runs have PostToolUse hooks enabled that automatically check files on Write/Edit:
- `.py` files: `python3 -m py_compile` for syntax errors
- `.sh` files: `bash -n` for syntax errors, `shellcheck` for lint
- `.ts` files: `bunx tsc --noEmit` for type errors
- `.ps1` files: `Invoke-ScriptAnalyzer` for PowerShell analysis
- `.yml` files in `.github/workflows/`: `actionlint` for workflow validation

Diagnostics appear as additional context before the agent's next turn, enabling faster error correction.

### Permissions

All tool permissions are bypassed (`--dangerously-skip-permissions`). Agents have full access to all tools without approval prompts.

### Execution

- Tasks run **sequentially** (one at a time).
- Each agent runs in an **isolated workspace directory** containing only a copy of this instructions file.
- No conversation context or prior run data is passed to the agent.

---

## Common Pitfalls

The following patterns were observed in v3 runs wasting significant agent time. Agents are encouraged to avoid them:

### PowerShell: Use `shell: pwsh` in workflow steps

When writing GitHub Actions workflow YAML for PowerShell mode, use `shell: pwsh` on your `run:` steps instead of invoking `pwsh -Command "..."` or `pwsh -File ...` from a bash shell. The `shell: pwsh` directive works correctly in act containers and avoids:
- Bash parser errors when PowerShell syntax (like `@'...'@` heredocs) leaks into bash
- Dollar-sign escaping issues (`\$` needed for every PS variable inside bash strings)
- Quoting/visibility problems when passing structured data (JSON) through bash to pwsh
- `$MyInvocation.ScriptName` being empty under `pwsh -File` invocation

Example -- prefer this:
```yaml
    - name: Run script
      shell: pwsh
      run: |
        ./MyScript.ps1 -Config config.json
```

Over this:
```yaml
    - name: Run script
      run: |
        pwsh -File ./MyScript.ps1 -Config config.json
```

### Act: Limit `act push` invocations

Each `act push` run takes 30-90 seconds (container startup + workflow execution). In v3, agents that ran `act push` more than twice spent 15+ minutes on repeated debugging loops. Strategy:
1. Get the workflow right with `actionlint` first (instant validation)
2. Run `act push` once to verify
3. If it fails, read the error carefully and fix the root cause before re-running
4. Avoid running `act push` more than 3 times total

### Fixtures: Design test data up front

Agents that wrote, broke, and rewrote test fixtures (4+ fixture-related commands) lost 1-5 minutes per run. Design your fixture data before writing tests -- think through the edge cases once, create all fixtures, then write tests against them.

### Test reruns: Fix the code, not the test command

Running the same test command 4+ times without changing the underlying code is a signal you're not diagnosing the root cause. Read the error output carefully, identify what's wrong, fix it, then re-run once.

### TypeScript: Expect type error hook cycles

The `tsc --noEmit` hook catches type errors on every Write/Edit. This is net-positive (53% catch rate) but each fix cycle takes ~12 seconds. To minimize cycles: use explicit types from the start, avoid `any`, and check your types mentally before writing.

### Workflow: Validate with actionlint before act

`actionlint` runs instantly and catches most YAML/syntax issues. Always run it before attempting `act push`. Agents that needed 3+ actionlint runs to pass wasted time on iterative fixes that could have been caught by reading the error messages more carefully.

---

## Tasks

### Task 11: Semantic Version Bumper
**Category**: GitHub Actions / Release Automation

Parse a version file (or package.json) containing a semantic version string, determine the next version based on conventional commit messages (feat -> minor, fix -> patch, breaking -> major), update the version file, generate a changelog entry from the commits, and output the new version. Create mock commit logs as test fixtures.

### Task 12: PR Label Assigner
**Category**: GitHub Actions / Triage

Given a list of changed file paths (simulating a PR's changed files), apply labels based on configurable path-to-label mapping rules (e.g., docs/** -> documentation, src/api/** -> api, *.test.* -> tests). Support glob patterns, multiple labels per file, and priority ordering when rules conflict. Output the final label set. Mock the file list for testing.

### Task 13: Dependency License Checker
**Category**: GitHub Actions / Compliance

Parse a dependency manifest (package.json, requirements.txt, or similar), extract dependency names and versions, check each against an allow-list and deny-list of licenses (provided as config), and generate a compliance report listing each dependency's license status (approved, denied, unknown). Mock the license lookup for testing.

### ~~Task 14: Docker Image Tag Generator~~ (Archived)
**Category**: GitHub Actions / CI/CD
**Status**: Archived after v4 — redundant with Task 16. See [archived-tasks/14-docker-image-tag-generator.md](archived-tasks/14-docker-image-tag-generator.md).

Given git context (branch name, commit SHA, tags, PR number -- all provided as mock inputs), generate appropriate Docker image tags following common conventions: latest for main, pr-{number} for PRs, v{semver} for tags, {branch}-{short-sha} for feature branches. Handle tag sanitization (lowercase, no special chars). Output the tag list.

### Task 15: Test Results Aggregator
**Category**: GitHub Actions / CI Reporting

Parse test result files in multiple formats (JUnit XML, and JSON), aggregate results across multiple files (simulating a matrix build), compute totals (passed, failed, skipped, duration), identify flaky tests (passed in some runs, failed in others), and generate a markdown summary suitable for a GitHub Actions job summary. Create sample test result files as fixtures.

### Task 16: Environment Matrix Generator
**Category**: GitHub Actions / CI Configuration

Given a configuration describing OS options, language versions, and feature flags, generate a build matrix (as JSON) suitable for GitHub Actions strategy.matrix. Support include/exclude rules, max-parallel limits, and fail-fast configuration. Validate that the matrix doesn't exceed a maximum size. Output the complete matrix JSON.

### Task 17: Artifact Cleanup Script
**Category**: GitHub Actions / Maintenance

Given a list of artifacts with metadata (name, size, creation date, workflow run ID -- provided as mock data), apply retention policies (max age, max total size, keep-latest-N per workflow), determine which artifacts to delete, and generate a deletion plan with a summary (total space reclaimed, artifacts retained vs deleted). Support dry-run mode.

### Task 18: Secret Rotation Validator
**Category**: GitHub Actions / Security

Given a configuration of secrets with metadata (name, last-rotated date, rotation policy in days, required-by services -- all mock data), identify secrets that are expired or expiring within a configurable warning window, generate a rotation report, and output notifications grouped by urgency (expired, warning, ok). Support multiple output formats (markdown table, JSON).

---

## Prompt Templates

### Default Mode
```

TASK: {task_description}

REQUIREMENTS:
1. Use red/green TDD methodology: write a failing test FIRST, then write the minimum code to make it pass, then refactor. Repeat for each piece of functionality.
2. Create mocks and test fixtures as necessary for testability.
3. All tests must be runnable and must pass at the end.
4. Include clear comments explaining your approach.
5. Handle errors gracefully with meaningful error messages.

Create your solution in the current working directory. Start by writing your first failing test.
```

6. Use `#!/usr/bin/env bash` shebang. Scripts must pass `shellcheck` and `bash -n` syntax validation.

5. Handle errors gracefully with meaningful error messages.
6. Use TypeScript features: explicit types, interfaces, and type annotations. Run scripts with `bun run <file>.ts`.

Create your solution in the current working directory. Start by writing your first failing test.
```

### GHA Workflow Addendum (appended to all prompts for tasks 11-18)

and works correctly in act containers. pwsh and Pester are pre-installed in the

WORKFLOW VALIDATION:

and fix any errors it reports. actionlint is pre-installed. Iterate until it passes cleanly.

WORKFLOW EXECUTION TEST (MANDATORY):

2. Runs: git add -A && git commit -m "test" && act push --rm 2>&1

The `act-result.txt` file MUST exist when your tests finish. It is a required artifact.

WORKFLOW STRUCTURE TESTS:

- Verify the workflow references your script files correctly (paths exist)
- Verify actionlint passes (run it as a subprocess in your test and assert exit code 0)

```

name: Secret Rotation Validator

on:
  push:
  pull_request:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 6 * * *"
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  validate-secrets:
    name: Validate Secret Rotation
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Bun
        run: |
          curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash
          echo "$HOME/.bun/bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: bun install

      - name: Run unit tests
        run: bun test src/validator.test.ts src/formatter.test.ts

      - name: Run validator - markdown report
        run: |
          echo "=== Secret Rotation Report (Markdown) ==="
          bun run src/index.ts --config fixtures/secrets.json --format markdown --date 2026-05-07

      - name: Run validator - JSON report
        run: |
          echo "=== Secret Rotation Report (JSON) ==="
          bun run src/index.ts --config fixtures/secrets.json --format json --date 2026-05-07

      - name: Check for expired secrets (fail if any)
        run: |
          OUTPUT=$(bun run src/index.ts --config fixtures/secrets.json --format json --date 2026-05-07)
          EXPIRED=$(echo "$OUTPUT" | grep '"expiredCount"' | grep -oP '\d+')
          echo "Expired secret count: $EXPIRED"
          if [ "$EXPIRED" -gt 0 ]; then
            echo "WARNING: $EXPIRED secret(s) are expired and must be rotated immediately!"
          fi

- `src/formatter.ts` — `formatMarkdown()` produces a GitHub-flavored markdown table document; `formatJSON()` produces pretty-printed JSON with a summary block
- `src/index.ts` — CLI with `--config`, `--format`, `--warning-window`, `--date` flags

- `.github/workflows/secret-rotation-validator.yml` — triggers on push/pull_request/schedule/dispatch; installs Bun via curl, runs unit tests, outputs both markdown and JSON reports, warns on expired secrets; passes actionlint cleanly
- `act-result.txt` — 83KB artifact with all act run output showing `Job succeeded` for both test cases
